stem: Let \( n = [[nval]] = [[d]] \cdot [[m]] \), and let \( I = ([[d]]) \) be the ideal of \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) generated by \( [[d]] \) (the multiples of \( [[d]] \) inside \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \)). By the correspondence theorem, \( \mathbb{Z}_{n}/I \cong \mathbb{Z}_{[[d]]} \). How many elements does the quotient ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{n}/I \) have?
solution: The ideal \( I=([[d]]) \) consists of the \( [[m]] \) multiples of \( [[d]] \) inside \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) (namely \( 0, [[d]], 2[[d]], \dots \)), so \( |I| = n/[[d]] = [[m]] \). By Lagrange, \( |\mathbb{Z}_{n}/I| = |\mathbb{Z}_{n}|/|I| = n/[[m]] = [[answer]] \); equivalently \( \mathbb{Z}_{n}/([[d]]) \cong \mathbb{Z}_{[[d]]} \), which has \( [[d]] \) elements.
distractor_rationales: This is \( |I| \), the size of the IDEAL itself (\( n/d \)), not the size of the quotient \( \mathbb{Z}_n/I \).
This is the size of the whole ring \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \), not the quotient by \( I \).
This divides \( n \) by \( d \), which actually gives \( |I| = m \), not \( |\mathbb{Z}_n/I| \).
This subtracts \( d \) from \( n \) for no valid reason connected to the quotient's size.
This is one more than the correct quotient size.
This is one fewer than the correct quotient size.
This adds an unrelated offset \( m \) to the correct quotient size.

stem: Let \( n = [[nval]] = [[d]] \cdot [[m]] \), and let \( I = ([[d]]) \) be the ideal of \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) generated by \( [[d]] \), so \( \mathbb{Z}_{n}/I \cong \mathbb{Z}_{[[d]]} \). By the R/I correspondence, \( I \) is a MAXIMAL ideal of \( \mathbb{Z}_{n} \) exactly when \( \mathbb{Z}_{n}/I \) is a field. Is \( I \) maximal? Enter \( 1 \) for yes, \( 0 \) for no.
solution: \( \mathbb{Z}_{n}/I \cong \mathbb{Z}_{[[d]]} \), and \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[d]]} \) is a field exactly when \( [[d]] \) is prime. So \( I=([[d]]) \) is maximal exactly when \( [[d]] \) is prime, giving the verdict \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the flipped verdict, swapping maximal and non-maximal.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.

stem: In the ring of Gaussian integers \( \mathbb{Z}[i] \), let \( I = ([[a]] + [[b]]i) \) be the principal ideal generated by \( [[a]]+[[b]]i \). It is a standard fact that \( |\mathbb{Z}[i]/I| \) equals the norm \( N([[a]]+[[b]]i) = [[a]]^{2} + [[b]]^{2} \). How many elements does the quotient ring \( \mathbb{Z}[i]/I \) have?
solution: The quotient of \( \mathbb{Z}[i] \) by the principal ideal generated by \( [[a]]+[[b]]i \) has order equal to the norm \( N([[a]]+[[b]]i) = [[a]]^{2}+[[b]]^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the real and imaginary parts instead of using the norm formula \( a^{2}+b^{2} \).
This multiplies the real and imaginary parts, not the norm formula.
This squares the SUM \( a+b \) instead of summing the squares \( a^{2}+b^{2} \).
This subtracts the squares instead of adding them, as in a difference-of-squares mixup.
This is one more than the correct quotient size.
This is one fewer than the correct quotient size.
This is two more than the correct quotient size.

stem: Let \( F = \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \), and let \( f(x) \in F[x] \) be an IRREDUCIBLE polynomial of degree \( [[k]] \). Let \( I = (f(x)) \) be the ideal of \( F[x] \) generated by \( f \), so \( F[x]/I \) is a field (since \( I \) is maximal exactly because \( f \) is irreducible). How many elements does the quotient field \( F[x]/I \) have?
solution: Elements of \( F[x]/(f) \) correspond bijectively to remainders upon division by the degree-\( [[k]] \) polynomial \( f \), i.e. polynomials of degree \( < [[k]] \) with coefficients in \( F \). There are \( [[p]] \) choices for each of the \( [[k]] \) coefficients, giving \( [[p]]^{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \) elements.
distractor_rationales: This multiplies \( p\cdot k \) instead of raising \( p \) to the power \( k \).
This swaps base and exponent, using \( k^{p} \) instead of \( p^{k} \).
This is \( p^{k}-1 \), the count of NONZERO elements (the multiplicative group), not the full quotient ring.
This uses one fewer remainder-coefficient slot than the degree \( [[k]] \) actually provides.
This is one more than the correct number of elements.
This is one fewer than the correct number of elements.
This adds an unrelated offset \( p \) to the correct number of elements.
